buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Slice then fill inplace — may not affect original
recent = df.loc['2020-01-01':]
recent.fillna(method='ffill', inplace=True)

# df still has NaN values from before 2020
print(df.isna().sum())
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('prices.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')

# Modify the original DataFrame directly
df.loc['2020-01-01':] = df.loc['2020-01-01':].ffill()

print(df.isna().sum())
description: inplace=True on a DataFrame slice may not propagate changes to the original.
explanation: When you slice a DataFrame with .loc[], pandas may return a view or a copy depending on the operation. Calling fillna(inplace=True) on a copy changes the copy, not the original. The original df still has NaN values. This is a common trap that produces silent incorrect results — no error is raised.

issues.title: inplace operation on slice may not modify original DataFrame
